body_txt: Fiction gives us many weddings. Sometimes, the couple is forced to Altar the Speed , maybe due to meddling parents or a baby on the way . The couple doesn't want to move the date up, but something or someone else is forcing them to do it. Sometimes, the couple just elopes, and avoids the big wedding mess altogether. But every now and then, a couple ends up having TWO weddings — one for themselves, and one for everyone else. This usually takes two forms: Elope First, Plan Later : The couple elopes, but before they can tell anyone, somebody says something to make them feel guilty that their friends and family weren't there to see it. So they end up planning a second wedding that everyone can attend. They might also do this for practical reasons: get legally married when it's convenient for the two of them and then have the Big Day when their schedules (and finances) allow. The couple will likely have to jump through hoops to prevent anyone from finding out they're already married, and the secret may or may not be spilled, but ultimately it won't matter. Plan First, Elope Later: The couple planned to have a big ceremony, but now everything is falling apart. They decide — spontaneously and usually simultaneously — to just elope and be done with it. Expect a very hasty ceremony at the office of the first Justice of the Peace they can find. They might confess afterward and call everything off, or they might regain their wedding spirit and actually start to enjoy planning the Big Day. In this case, the secret is almost never revealed. This trope is almost always a Heartwarming Moment . The couple is showing that the marriage is far more important to them than the wedding, and they refuse to get sidetracked by a million questions about bouquets and seating assignments. They may still do the big wedding, but it'll be something they do for their family and friends; the real ceremony will always be the one which was just between them. Both types are a major Truth in Television . The Trope name is a play on the title of the film My Own Private Idaho , which has absolutely nothing to do with this trope. See also Altar the Speed , Metaphorical Marriage and Elopement .
